Manager Briefing — Talking Points
Pre-Announcement Q&A Guide for Leadership
Company
{{companyName}}
Date
{{sentDate}}
Prepared For
{{recipientName}}
Reference
{{ref}}
Confidential: This document is for {{companyName}} leadership only. Do not distribute to employees before the official announcement. These talking points have been reviewed by legal counsel and the ESOP trustee.
§ 01Key Messages

When speaking with employees, lead with these five core messages. Every answer should reinforce at least one of them.

1.The company is not being sold. {{companyName}} remains an independent, privately held company. There is no outside buyer, no merger, and no layoffs planned as a result of this transition.
2.Your job is secure. The ESOP is designed to preserve the company and its workforce. The management team stays in place. Operations continue as normal.
3.You are now an owner. Every eligible employee will receive beneficial ownership in {{companyName}} through the ESOP — at no cost to you. This is not a stock purchase program.
4.This is a retirement benefit. The ESOP is a qualified retirement plan under federal law, similar in structure to a 401(k). Your account grows as the company grows.
5.Day-to-day operations do not change. Your job, your manager, your pay, your benefits — none of that changes because of the ESOP. The change is that you now share in the company's success.
§ 02What We Can Say

You are approved to share the following with employees. Use plain language — avoid jargon.

A.What an ESOP is. "An ESOP is a retirement plan that gives you ownership in the company. The company contributes shares to a trust on your behalf. You don't pay anything — it's a benefit the company provides."
B.How accounts work. "You'll receive an account that holds shares of {{companyName}} stock. The value of your account depends on how much the company is worth. As the company performs better, your account grows."
C.When accounts vest. "Your account vests over time according to the plan's vesting schedule. Once you're fully vested, the entire account balance is yours. The vesting schedule is outlined in the plan document you'll receive."
D.What happens at distribution. "When you leave the company — whether by retirement, disability, or other separation — you'll receive the vested value of your account. The plan has a distribution policy that determines timing and payment methods."
E.Tax advantages. "ESOP accounts grow tax-deferred. You won't pay taxes on the value of your account until you receive a distribution, which can be rolled over into an IRA."
§ 03What We Cannot Say

The following topics are off-limits. If an employee asks about any of these, use the escalation protocol in §05.

Do not discuss: Specific stock values, appraised price per share, or what an individual employee's account is worth. Valuation is determined annually by an independent appraiser.
Do not promise: Future stock performance, guaranteed returns, specific payout amounts, or timelines for liquidity events. ESOP accounts are subject to market risk like any ownership.
Do not compare: The ESOP to publicly traded stocks, 401(k) matching, or other investment vehicles. These are different structures with different rules.
Do not advise: On individual tax implications, retirement planning, or financial decisions. Employees should consult their own tax or financial advisor.
Do not speculate: On the company's future sale, IPO, or any transaction that might affect share value. The ESOP is a long-term ownership structure, not a short-term exit strategy.
§ 04Anticipated Questions & Answers

These are the most common employee questions. Use these responses as a starting point — adapt to the conversation.

Am I getting a pay cut?
No. Your compensation — salary, wages, and existing benefits — is not affected by the ESOP. The ESOP is an additional retirement benefit on top of what you already receive.
Do I have to buy shares or put in my own money?
No. This is not a stock purchase plan. The company contributes shares to your account at no cost to you. You never write a check or have money deducted from your paycheck for the ESOP.
What if I leave the company?
When you leave, you'll receive the vested portion of your account. The distribution timing depends on the plan's distribution policy — typically within a year of separation. You can also roll the distribution into an IRA to continue tax-deferred growth.
When do I get my money?
ESOP accounts are long-term retirement benefits. Distributions generally occur when you retire, become disabled, or otherwise separate from the company. The plan has a vesting schedule, and distributions follow the rules outlined in the plan document.
Is my 401(k) affected?
No. The ESOP is a separate retirement plan. Your 401(k) — if you have one — continues as it always has. The ESOP does not replace or modify any existing benefit plan.
Who runs the company now?
The same management team that has been running the company. The ESOP does not change the day-to-day leadership, organizational structure, or decision-making. The owner has transitioned ownership to employees but remains involved in the business {{ownerRole}}.
How much is my account worth?
The value of your account is determined by an independent annual appraisal of the company's fair market value. You will receive an annual statement showing your account balance. We cannot share specific values before the appraisal is complete.
Is the ESOP guaranteed by the government?
The ESOP is a qualified retirement plan regulated under federal ERISA law, which provides important protections. However, like any ownership stake, the value depends on the company's performance. The Department of Labor oversees ESOP compliance.
§ 05Escalation Protocol

If an employee asks a question you cannot answer, do not guess. Use this protocol:

A.Benefits & plan details — direct to HR or the plan administrator. They have the plan document and can answer vesting schedules, eligibility, and distribution rules.
B.Transaction process & timeline — direct to {{forhemitContact}} at Forhemit. They can explain the stewardship process and what to expect next.
C.Fiduciary & trust matters — direct to the independent ESOP trustee, {{trusteeName}}. They are responsible for protecting employee interests and can address concerns about governance.
D.Tax & personal finance — advise the employee to consult their own tax advisor or financial planner. Do not provide tax or investment advice.
E.Media inquiries — direct any media questions to {{mediaContact}}. No employee should speak to press on behalf of the company without approval.
When in doubt: Say "That's a great question — let me get you the right answer from the right person." Then escalate. It is always better to follow up than to give incorrect information.
§ 06Meeting Logistics

Details for the employee announcement meeting:

All-hands meeting, in-person preferred
{{announcementDuration}}

Distribution materials at the meeting:

2.Welcome to Your ESOP booklet
3.Trustee welcome letter
4.ESOP FAQ sheet
5.Contact card for follow-up questions
—Forhemit Transition Stewardship
